title: The EOFY Number Your Bank Cares About More Than Your Profit
linkedin:
Your end-of-year profit figure will satisfy the ATO.
It will not necessarily satisfy your bank.

When commercial lenders reassess a business at EOFY, they are reading the balance sheet to check whether your assets and liabilities still support the facilities you hold. The P&L tells them what you earned. The balance sheet tells them whether the business can carry what it owes.

The metric they focus on is the current ratio: current assets divided by current liabilities. Most commercial lenders require at least 1.2 before extending or renewing a working capital facility. Below 1.0, and the picture looks constrained.

They also read the debtors ledger in detail. A business with $800,000 in receivables and $300,000 of that sitting past 60 days looks very different to a lender than one with the same total and a clean ageing profile. Quality matters as much as quantity.

Several practical steps remain available before June 30. Collecting aged debtors, avoiding unnecessary draws on revolving facilities in the final days of the month, and settling near-term supplier obligations where cash supports it can all shift the year-end picture before the snapshot is taken.

With clean EOFY accounts in hand, the weeks after June 30 are also the strongest moment to review your banking arrangements. Most business owners have not looked at their facility pricing or structure since the original application. For a business that has grown or strengthened its balance sheet, that gap is often worth closing.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#FinancialStrategy #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness

June 30 is almost here. Most business owners are focused on profit. But if your business carries an overdraft, a trade finance line, or equipment debt, your bank is reading something else entirely: your balance sheet.

What does your current ratio look like right now?

The latest ProfitPulse post covers what lenders actually check in your EOFY accounts, the specific ratios that matter, and the steps still available before June 30 to improve the picture.

google:
As the financial year draws to a close, most business owners on the East Coast are focused on the profit figure. But if your business carries a bank facility of any kind, your lender is focused on something different: your balance sheet.

Commercial lenders assess their exposure at EOFY by reviewing your current ratio, the ageing of your debtors, and your debt service coverage ratio. These are the numbers that determine whether your facilities renew cleanly or trigger a conversation you were not expecting.

Practical steps remain available before June 30. A focused effort on collecting debtors aged beyond 60 days, avoiding draws on revolving credit at month end, and settling short-term supplier obligations can improve the year-end picture before the snapshot is taken.

After June 30, with fresh twelve-month accounts available, it is also the strongest time to review whether your facilities are still priced and structured correctly. Many Brisbane, Queensland, and NSW businesses find their arrangements reflect a version of the business from several years ago. That gap is worth closing.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led businesses across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria at exactly this point in the year. Read the full post for the specific ratios to check and the decisions still available before the financial year closes.

https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-eofy-number-your-bank-cares-about-more-than-your-profit
